rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical azidocyanation of alkene-tethered cyanohydrins via Mn-mediated azide radical addition and 1,4-nitrile migration to deliver 1,2-azidonitriles.
electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of an in situ formed [Mn(II)X2N3]- complex to a Mn(III)-azide species that delivers an azidyl radical to the alkene; cathode reduces protons to H2.

key_intermediates.name: secondary alkyl C-radical from azide addition to alkene (36)
cyclic iminyl radical from intramolecular nitrile cyclization (37)

electron_flow: At the anode, Mn(II)/azide ate complex is oxidized to Mn(III)-N3, which transfers an azidyl radical to the alkene of the cyanohydrin to give a C-centered radical; intramolecular addition onto the pendant nitrile forms an iminyl radical that undergoes beta-scission to a ketyl-type alkyl radical, which is anodically oxidized and loses H+ to give the 1,2-azidonitrile, while protons are reduced to H2 at the Pt cathode.

probe_reagents.name: cyclopropyl-substituted cyanohydrin (radical clock 34)
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-opened azido-nitrile 35 obtained as sole product in 56% NMR yield, consistent with a carbon-radical intermediate after azide addition.

probe_reagents.name: Mn(OTf)2 / TBAN3 cyclic voltammetry
probe_reagents.observation: TBAN3 alone shows irreversible oxidation at ~0.97 V vs Fc/Fc+; with Mn(OTf)2 a new irreversible event appears at ~0.50 V vs Fc/Fc+, indicating formation of an oxidizable [Mn(II)X2N3]- species (Mn(II)->Mn(III)).

notes: Authors note azidyl radicals could in principle be generated directly by anodic oxidation of azide, but CV shows Mn(OTf)2 lowers the oxidation potential substantially, supporting a Mn-mediated inner-sphere azide-transfer pathway rather than free N3 radical from direct azide oxidation. Methodology extends to sulfonylcyanation (using NaSO2Ph) via the same radical/migration manifold and to a malononitrile-derived substrate (30 -> 31).
